channel_title: AI News & Strategy Daily | Nate B Jones
title: Why Your AI Business Writing Sucks (It's Not AI!) #aiwriting #workwithai #promptengineering #llm
executive_summary: In the video, Nate B. Jones explains that AI-generated documents are overwhelming teams because they lack clear standards and goals. He emphasizes that teams with defined quality criteria will excel, while those without will struggle, leading to 'AI slop' in their outputs.
bullets: AI-generated documents are overwhelming teams, leading to confusion and inefficiency. This is a symptom of unclear thinking and standards, not just a flaw in AI. It's like trying to build a house without a blueprint — you end up with a structure that might collapse.

Defining concrete quality criteria is essential for effective AI writing. Without these standards, AI can produce outputs that are irrelevant or poorly structured. Think of it like setting a recipe for a cake; if you don't specify the ingredients and measurements, the cake could end up inedible.

Intent-driven writing is a crucial concept for using AI effectively. This means writing with a clear purpose and audience in mind, which helps guide the AI in generating relevant content. It's like giving a GPS a clear destination; without it, you might end up lost.

Using AI for evaluation can enhance the writing process. Instead of just relying on AI to generate text, teams should also use it to assess the quality of their writing against set standards. Imagine using a coach to review your game performance; feedback helps you improve.

Teams that articulate clear standards and goals will thrive with AI; those that don't will drown in 'AI slop.' This highlights the importance of clarity in communication and expectations. It's like a ship navigating through fog; without a clear course, it risks crashing.

Many teams currently have a backward approach to evaluation, focusing too much on functionality rather than quality. For instance, they might spend 80% of their time checking if the code works, rather than ensuring it meets hygiene standards. Think of it like a car that runs but has a dirty engine; it won't last long.

To improve AI writing, teams should implement a rule that at least 50% of evaluation tests check for code hygiene. This ensures that the writing is not only functional but also clean and maintainable. It's like making sure your kitchen is clean before cooking; a clean space leads to better meals.

Nate suggests that teams should spend 4-5 months refining their writing processes to integrate AI effectively. This timeline allows for thorough training and adjustment to new standards. Like training for a marathon, you need time to build endurance and skill.

AI slop is not just a nuisance; it can kill businesses by wasting time and resources. Poorly written documents can lead to misunderstandings and lost opportunities. It's like trying to sell a product with a broken advertisement; no one will buy it.

The common misconception is that AI is solely to blame for poor writing. In reality, it often reveals pre-existing issues in clarity and communication within teams. Think of it like a mirror reflecting your appearance; if you look messy, it's not the mirror's fault.

Nate emphasizes the need for teams to establish a culture of clear communication and standards. This cultural shift is necessary for leveraging AI effectively. It's like planting a garden; without proper care and guidelines, the plants won't thrive.

Effective AI writing requires continuous feedback and iteration. Teams should regularly review and adjust their standards based on AI outputs and team performance. It's like tuning a musical instrument; regular adjustments lead to harmonious results.
key_takeaways: Set a rule that at least 50% of your evaluation tests must check for code hygiene, not just functionality. This shift will improve the quality of your outputs and prevent future issues. If you skip this, you risk producing subpar work that could harm your reputation.

Spend 4-5 months refining your writing processes to integrate AI effectively. This timeline allows for thorough training and adjustment to new standards, ensuring your team is prepared. If you neglect this, you may find your team struggling to keep up with AI advancements.

Define concrete quality criteria for your writing tasks. This will help guide AI outputs and ensure they meet your expectations. Without these criteria, you risk generating irrelevant or poorly structured content.

technical_terms.AI slop: The poor quality output generated when AI is used without clear standards or goals. Think of it like a messy room; without organization, everything just piles up and becomes unusable.
technical_terms.Intent-driven writing: Writing that is focused on a specific purpose and audience, guiding the AI to produce relevant content. Like a well-aimed arrow, it hits the target more accurately when there's a clear aim.
technical_terms.Quality criteria: Specific standards that define what constitutes good writing, which AI can follow to generate better outputs. Think of it like a checklist for a flight; without it, you might miss crucial steps before takeoff.
technical_terms.Evaluation tests: Assessments used to check the quality and effectiveness of writing or code. Like a quality control check in a factory, these tests ensure the final product meets the required standards.
technical_terms.Code hygiene: The practice of maintaining clean, organized, and efficient code. It's like keeping your workspace tidy; a clean environment leads to better productivity and fewer mistakes.
technical_terms.Feedback loop: A system where outputs are reviewed and used to improve future performance. Like a coach reviewing game footage with players, it helps identify areas for improvement.
technical_terms.Cultural shift: A change in the way a team communicates and sets standards. Think of it like a team learning to play a new sport; everyone must adapt to succeed together.
technical_terms.Integration: The process of combining AI tools into existing workflows. Like adding a new ingredient to a recipe, it requires careful adjustment to ensure the final dish is successful.
technical_terms.Iteration: The repeated process of refining and improving outputs based on feedback. Like sculpting a statue, each pass refines the shape until it becomes a masterpiece.
technical_terms.Training period: A designated time for teams to learn and adapt to new tools or processes. Think of it like a boot camp; intensive training prepares you for the challenges ahead.
